column6: Product security checks must inspect only the configured product repository and must not scan unrelated directories.
Secret-like values, private keys, credentials, tokens, or private messages must not be committed, printed, or logged.
Environment files should be ignored or documented; warnings must not hide high-confidence secret exposure.
External integrations require explicit service, data, OAuth, token, webhook, logging, rate-limit, sandbox, and rollback confirmation before implementation.
Dependency, authentication, API, storage, deletion, or logging changes require security-oriented review.
Product work must preserve existing Git sync, CI, workflow-pair, and repository-boundary gates.
Product security must keep advisory warnings separate from high-confidence blocking failures.
Product security output may show categories and file references, but must not print secret values.

Parent document-sync enforcement must not expand product registry paths, scan child repositories, invoke child tests, use network services, or treat external evidence as a parent synchronization substitute.
Verification receipts must not contain raw output, environment dumps, secrets, credentials, repository URLs, or host-specific absolute paths.
Composed verification must use validated argv, bounded output, configured timeouts and locks, and process-group cancellation without granting external-repository or destructive authority.
Dashboard build and browser success may be composed only within one run when revision, policy, command, source, output, and test inventory digests match exactly.
Dashboard build caches and other mutable tool state must remain outside immutable source and configuration inventories so clean-run verification cannot alter its own validated inputs.
Distributed CI results may satisfy a final gate only through exact semantic ownership and a proof bound to the current workflow graph, policy, catalog, revision, and provider results.
